section.title: Contend with Those Who Contend
section.sectionRationale: An imprecatory lament of David, appealing to the LORD as both warrior and judge to contend with those who repay his kindness with betrayal and hate him without cause, a phrase Jesus applies to His own rejection in John 15:25. David's honest cry for justice is not personal vengeance but a plea entrusting judgment entirely to God.
section.thesis: David's plea that the LORD contend with those who hate him without cause, though he had shown them only compassion, is an honest, Spirit-given prayer entrusting justice to God rather than seizing it himself, a pattern of innocent suffering fulfilled in Jesus, hated without cause by those He came to save.
section.frameNote: Six movements: an opening plea that the LORD contend as warrior for him (1-3); prayer for the humiliation of those who plotted evil without cause (4-8); a vow to rejoice in the LORD's salvation (9-10); unjust accusation despite his compassion in their illness (11-16); the cry 'how long, O LORD,' and prayer against those who hate without cause (17-21); and a closing appeal for vindication and a vow of ongoing praise (22-28).

units.label: Contend with my opponents, O LORD
units.note: an opening plea for God as warrior

verses.text: {{riyb|Contend}} with my opponents, O LORD; fight against those who fight against me.
Take up Your shield and buckler; arise and come to my aid.
Draw the spear and javelin against my pursuers; say to my soul: "I am your salvation."

units.label: Without cause they laid their net
units.note: prayer for the humiliation of hidden plots

verses.text: May those who seek my life be disgraced and put to shame; may those who plan to harm me be driven back and confounded.
May they be like chaff in the wind, as the angel of the LORD drives them away.
May their path be dark and slick, as the angel of the LORD pursues.
For without cause they laid their net for me; without reason they dug a pit for my soul.

May ruin befall them by surprise; may the net they hid ensnare them; 
may they fall into the hazard they created.

units.label: My soul will rejoice in the LORD
units.note: a vow to rejoice in salvation

verses.text: Then my soul will rejoice in the LORD and exult in His salvation.
All my bones will exclaim, "Who is like You, O LORD, who delivers the afflicted from the aggressor, the poor and needy from the robber?"

units.label: They repay me evil for good

verses.text: Hostile witnesses come forward; they make charges I know nothing about.
They repay me evil for good, to the bereavement of my soul.
Yet when they were ill, I put on sackcloth; I humbled myself with fasting, but my prayers returned unanswered.
I paced about as for my friend or brother; I was bowed down with grief, like one mourning for his mother.
But when I stumbled, they assembled in glee; they gathered together against me. Assailants I did not know slandered me without ceasing.
Like godless jesters at a feast, they gnashed their teeth at me.

units.label: How long, O Lord
units.note: a cry against those who hate without cause

verses.text:
How long, O Lord, will You look on? Rescue my soul from their ravages, 
my precious life from these lions.
Then I will give You thanks in the great assembly; I will praise You among many people.
Let not my enemies gloat over me {{chinnam|without cause}}, nor those who hate me without reason wink in malice.

For they do not speak peace, but they devise deceitful schemes 
against those who live quietly in the land.
They gape at me and say, "Aha, aha! Our eyes have seen!"

units.label: Vindicate me by Your righteousness
units.note: a closing appeal and vow of praise

verses.text: O LORD, You have seen it; be not silent. O Lord, be not far from me.
Awake and rise to my defense, to my cause, my God and my Lord!
Vindicate me by Your righteousness, O LORD my God, and do not let them gloat over me.
Let them not say in their hearts, "Aha, just what we wanted!" Let them not say, "We have swallowed him up!"
May those who gloat in my distress be ashamed and confounded; may those who exalt themselves over me be clothed in shame and reproach.
May those who favor my vindication shout for joy and gladness; may they always say, "Exalted be the LORD who delights in His servant's well-being."
Then my tongue will proclaim Your {{tsedeq|righteousness}} and Your praises all day long.

riyb.gloss: To contend, dispute, plead a legal case. David asks the LORD to act as both warrior and advocate, taking up his cause the way a defender argues a case in court, a plea for justice rather than personal revenge.

chinnam.gloss: Without cause, for nothing, gratuitously. David's repeated insistence that his enemies hate him without cause becomes, in Jesus's own words, a direct description of His rejection by the world, John 15:25.

tsedeq.gloss: Righteousness, rightness, justice. David's closing vow is to proclaim this quality of God continually, the same righteousness ultimately vindicated when the truly innocent sufferer, Christ, was raised from the dead.

basic.context: David appeals to the LORD as both warrior and legal defender, asking Him to contend with those who plot against him without cause even though he had shown them only kindness, fasting and mourning when they were ill. Betrayed by hostile witnesses and mocked by those gloating over his distress, David cries out, 'How long, O Lord, will You look on,' and pleads for vindication by God's own righteousness, vowing to praise Him publicly once justice is done.
basic.hermeneutics: This imprecatory psalm should be read as honest, Spirit-given prayer that entrusts vengeance entirely to God rather than seizing it personally, echoing Romans 12:19's command to leave room for God's wrath rather than repaying evil directly.
basic.originalLanguages: David asks the LORD to riyb, contend or plead his legal case, against enemies who oppose him chinnam, without cause, a phrase Jesus later applies directly to His own rejection, John 15:25, and vows to proclaim God's tsedeq, righteousness, once vindicated.
basic.typology: David's experience of being hated without cause despite showing only compassion, verses 12-16, 19, anticipates the greater innocent sufferer, Jesus, who was likewise hated without cause by those He came to heal and save, a connection Jesus Himself makes explicit, John 15:25.
basic.christ: Jesus quotes this psalm's language directly, applying David's cry that his enemies 'hate me without reason' to His own rejection by the world: 'the word that is written in their Law must be fulfilled: They hated Me without cause,' John 15:25. Where David could only appeal to God to contend for him and vindicate him by righteousness, Christ's own vindication came through resurrection, the ultimate proof that the truly innocent sufferer is upheld by God even when hated and betrayed by those He loved.
basic.god: God is shown as both warrior and judge, one who sees every hidden plot and unjust accusation, and whose righteousness is the only sufficient ground for vindicating the innocent sufferer, sparing David the need to take vengeance into his own hands.
basic.crossReferences: John 15:25 quotes this psalm's language directly, applying 'hated Me without a cause' to Jesus's rejection by the world. Romans 12:19 commands believers to leave room for God's wrath rather than avenging themselves, the same posture David models throughout this psalm. Isaiah 53:7 pictures the suffering servant, silent before His accusers, embodying the innocent suffering this psalm describes.
basic.oneStory: David's honest cry that he is hated without cause despite showing only compassion becomes, in Jesus's own words, a description of His own rejection by the world, and just as David entrusted his vindication entirely to God's righteousness, Christ's resurrection is the final proof that the truly innocent sufferer is upheld by God, never abandoned to His enemies' gloating.

context.literary: An imprecatory lament of David combining courtroom and battlefield imagery, moving through repeated cycles of plea, vow to praise, renewed complaint, and closing appeal, a pattern showing the raw, repetitive honesty of prayer under sustained injustice.
context.historical: Traditionally attributed to David amid betrayal by those he had previously shown kindness, possibly during Saul's persecution or Absalom's rebellion, when former allies turned to slander and false accusation against him.
context.canonical: Belongs to the imprecatory psalm tradition, alongside Psalm 69 and Psalm 109, which the New Testament reads not as models of personal vengeance but as patterns of entrusting justice to God, fulfilled uniquely in Christ's own experience of being hated without cause, John 15:25.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring honest, unfiltered lament over betrayal and injustice before God, appealing to His righteous character for vindication rather than seizing vengeance personally, and to vow public praise once that vindication comes.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific experience of betrayal and his prayer for justice; it functions prescriptively as a pattern for believers facing similar injustice to entrust vengeance to God, echoed directly in Romans 12:19, rather than to take retaliation into their own hands.

debates.question: Is it appropriate for New Testament believers to pray imprecatory prayers like this psalm's requests for enemies' shame and ruin, or should such prayers be read as belonging to the old covenant's framework of this-worldly justice, now transposed by Jesus's command to love and pray for enemies, Matthew 5:44?

views.label: Imprecatory psalms like this one remain a legitimate model for believers today when facing persistent, unrepentant injustice, since they express not personal revenge but a handing over of judgment entirely to God, consistent with Romans 12:19's command to leave room for God's wrath rather than avenging oneself
views.case: David never takes vengeance into his own hands throughout the psalm; every request is addressed to God as the one who must act, modeling exactly the posture Paul commends, entrusting justice to God's righteous judgment rather than to human retaliation, a pattern still needed whenever believers face real, ongoing injustice.

views.label: While the psalm's underlying honesty and appeal to God's justice remain valuable, the specific content of praying for enemies' shame and ruin belongs most naturally to the old covenant's this-worldly justice, and Jesus's command to love enemies and pray for those who persecute us, Matthew 5:44, suggests believers today should transpose such prayers toward enemies' repentance and salvation rather than their downfall
views.case: The New Testament ethic explicitly commands love and blessing toward persecutors rather than imprecation, and Jesus Himself, hated without cause as this very psalm anticipates, responded not with imprecation but with forgiveness from the cross, suggesting a fulfilled pattern that reshapes how such prayers should be prayed after Christ.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that these prayers were never a license for personal vengeance, but always an appeal to God's own justice; the question is simply whether believers today should pray the psalm's specific content directly against persecutors or transpose its honesty toward prayer for their repentance, following Christ's own example of both truthful lament and costly love.

study.originalLanguages: David asks the LORD to riyb, contend, as his legal advocate against enemies who oppose him chinnam, without cause, the very phrase Jesus applies to His own rejection, John 15:25, and vows to proclaim God's tsedeq, righteousness, once vindicated.

typology.type: David hated without cause despite showing only compassion, verses 12-19
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, hated without cause by those He came to heal and save, John 15:25.

typology.type: David's appeal to be vindicated by God's righteousness, verse 24
typology.fulfillment: Christ's resurrection, the ultimate vindication of the truly innocent sufferer.

study.christ: Jesus quotes this psalm's language directly of His own experience: 'They hated Me without a cause,' John 15:25, identifying His own rejection by the world with David's cry in verse 19. Where David could only appeal to God to contend for him and vindicate him by righteousness, Christ's vindication came through His resurrection, proof that the truly innocent sufferer, hated without cause, is upheld by God even when abandoned and betrayed by those He loved.
study.god: God is shown as both warrior and judge, seeing every hidden plot and false accusation, whose righteousness alone is sufficient ground for vindicating the innocent, sparing His people the burden of taking vengeance into their own hands.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes this psalm's language directly, 'They hated Me without a cause,' applying David's cry to His own rejection by the world.
Believers are commanded to leave room for God's wrath rather than avenging themselves, the same posture David models throughout this psalm.
The suffering servant, silent before His accusers, embodies the innocent suffering this psalm describes and anticipates.

study.oneStory: David's honest cry that he is hated without cause despite showing only compassion becomes, in Jesus's own words, a description of His own rejection by the world. Just as David entrusted his vindication entirely to God's righteousness, Christ's resurrection is the final proof that the innocent sufferer is upheld by God, never abandoned to His enemies' gloating.

quiz.q: What imagery does David use in verses 1-3 to describe his plea to God?

options.text: Both courtroom and battlefield imagery, asking God to contend as his advocate and fight as his warrior.
options.note: David combines legal and military language, asking the LORD to take up shield and spear on his behalf.

options.text: Only military imagery, with no legal or courtroom language.
options.note: Close, since battle imagery, shield and spear, is prominent, but the opening verb 'contend' also carries specific legal, courtroom overtones alongside the military language.

options.text: Only agricultural imagery of planting and harvest.
options.note: The imagery here is military and legal, not agricultural.

options.text: Imagery of a wedding celebration.
options.note: The opening imagery concerns conflict and legal defense, not celebration.

quiz.q: How does David say he treated his enemies when they were ill, according to verses 13-14?

options.text: He put on sackcloth, fasted, and mourned for them as for a close friend or family member.
options.note: David describes genuine compassion shown to those who later betrayed him.

options.text: He felt sympathy privately but took no visible action.
options.note: Close, since sympathy is part of the picture, but David describes active, visible practices, sackcloth and fasting, not merely private feeling.

options.text: He ignored them completely.
options.note: David describes active mourning and fasting on their behalf, not indifference.

options.text: He publicly celebrated their suffering.
options.note: David's response was mourning and intercession, not celebration of their suffering.

quiz.q: What phrase in verse 19 does Jesus later apply directly to Himself in John 15:25?

options.text: 'Those who hate me without reason.'
options.note: Jesus quotes this language directly to describe His own rejection by the world.

options.text: 'My precious life from these lions.'
options.note: While vivid, this is not the specific phrase Jesus quotes; the direct quotation concerns being hated without cause.

options.text: 'They repay me evil for good.'
options.note: Close in theme, since both phrases describe unjust treatment, but the exact wording Jesus quotes in John 15:25 is 'hated Me without a cause,' from verse 19, not the 'evil for good' language of verse 12.

options.text: 'They gape at me and say, Aha, aha!'
options.note: This is not the phrase Jesus quotes; the direct quotation is about being hated without cause.

quiz.q: What does David ask God to do in verse 24?

options.text: Vindicate him by God's own righteousness, and not let his enemies gloat over him.
options.note: David appeals specifically to God's righteous character as the ground for his vindication.

options.text: Simply remove him from the situation without addressing the accusers.
options.note: Close, since relief is desired, but verse 24 specifically asks God to actively vindicate him by righteousness and stop the enemies' gloating, not merely to remove him from the circumstance.

options.text: Remove him from public life entirely.
options.note: The request is for vindication and God's active defense, not withdrawal from public life.

options.text: Punish David for his own past failures instead.
options.note: The request concerns vindication against false accusers, not self-punishment.

quiz.q: What does David vow to do once vindicated, according to verse 28?

options.text: Proclaim God's righteousness and praises all day long.
options.note: The psalm closes with a vow of continual, public praise once vindication comes.

options.text: Give thanks privately, without any public declaration.
options.note: Close, since gratitude is the underlying attitude, but verse 28 specifically vows public, ongoing proclamation, 'my tongue will proclaim,' not private, silent thanks.

options.text: Seek out his enemies to personally punish them.
options.note: The closing vow is about praise, not personal retaliation against enemies.

options.text: Remain silent about what God has done.
options.note: The vow is explicitly to proclaim, not to remain silent.

questions: David shows genuine compassion, fasting and mourning, for enemies who later betray him. Have you ever been mistreated by someone you had genuinely cared for, and how did you respond?

This imprecatory psalm entrusts vengeance entirely to God rather than seizing it personally. Where in your own life are you tempted to take justice into your own hands rather than trusting God's timing and righteousness?

Jesus applies this psalm's words, 'hated without cause,' directly to His own rejection. How does knowing that Jesus experienced the same kind of unjust hatred you may face change the way you process being wrongly treated?

David cries, 'How long, O Lord, will You look on?' in the middle of ongoing injustice. What situation in your life currently tempts you to ask God this same question, and how are you bringing that honestly to Him?
David vows to praise God publicly once vindicated. What would it look like for you to praise God now, in the middle of unresolved injustice, rather than waiting until every wrong is made right?

translationNotes.note: Contend renders riyb, a term used both for military conflict and formal legal disputes, picturing God as both warrior and advocate for the one who cannot defend himself.
Without cause renders chinnam, quoted directly by Jesus in John 15:25 to describe His own rejection by those He came to save.

Gloat and confounded describe the reversal David prays for, that those who rejoiced in his distress would themselves experience shame, a plea for justice rather than a claim of personal authority to enact it.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 35:1-28. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was found specifically expositing Psalm 35 in searches conducted this session, so sermon status is none.
